spec_match.rationale: The two header panels accurately reflect the asymmetry: source headers still cap at 25, trigger headers do not. Header names and payload shapes are correct.

interactive.rationale: The only interactive surface is a boilerplate 'live probe' that detects unrelated platform APIs (CSS.supports, navigator.gpu, navigator.credentials) and a 'toggle comparison state' that just flips a CSS class. Neither probe relates to Attribution Reporting; the rest of the page is static JSON snippets. This is a styled code-card.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Feature index blurb promises a 'side-by-side' header explorer showing where the cap applies and where it doesn't, which the static panels do deliver — but the blurb implies real exploration that the page does not provide.

edge_cases.rationale: No mechanism to type / paste a key and watch the verdict flip; no demonstration of the storage-quota motivation, no aggregatable_values cap behaviour, no error response from a too-long source key. Purely happy path.

references.rationale: see also block links to other internal concepts but never to https://chromestatus.com/feature/5131578140000256 or the WICG spec — the only chromestatus link on this concept page is missing, breaking rule #3 of CLAUDE.md.

design_system.rationale: Uses CSS variables and panels correctly, but the inline h3 uses inline style attributes instead of class hooks, and the boilerplate live-probe panel introduces classes (.is-compared) with no corresponding CSS so the toggle is invisible.

openQuestions.title: Add the missing chromestatus.com reference link
openQuestions.detail: Per CLAUDE.md rule #3, every page must link to https://chromestatus.com/feature/5131578140000256 — currently the see-also list only points at sibling concept pages, which means the fix-slugs recovery script cannot recover this folder if it gets renamed. Add the canonical reference link plus the WICG spec URL.

openQuestions.title: Replace the boilerplate live-probe with a real header verdict tool
openQuestions.detail: The current 'live probe' checks CSS.supports/navigator.gpu/etc which is irrelevant to attribution reporting. Replace it with an input that lets the visitor paste an arbitrary aggregation key name, and emit two verdict pills (source rejects vs trigger accepts). That makes the header asymmetry feel real instead of static.

openQuestions.title: Demonstrate the rejection response
openQuestions.detail: Show what Chrome actually does when a source registration ships a > 25 char key — a console warning, a report dropped, or a header validation error. Pair that with the trigger side accepting silently. The blunt before/after framing currently hides the failure mode.

summary: Page does the spec framing accurately but the interactivity is a boilerplate probe of unrelated APIs and the canonical chromestatus reference link is missing. Both should be fixed before this counts as a real concept demo.
